response.explanation:
Continuing Airworthiness Management Responsibilities The continuing airworthiness management system (CAMO) is responsible for ensuring that all aircraft remain safe, compliant, and fit for operation. The key responsibilities, drawn from the retrieved material, are organized below: | Area | Responsibility | Key Points | |------|----------------|------------| | Nominated Persons & Management Personnel | • The nominated postholder for continuing airworthiness (as required by M.A.706(d)) must ensure that all maintenance is performed on time and to an approved standard.
• This person must have corporate authority to finance and carry out all continuing‑airworthiness activities.
• The group of persons (M.A.706(c)) that support the nominated postholder must cover all duties described in Part‑M. | • For small operators the nominated postholder may also be the sole member of the group. | | Manpower & Training | • The CAMO must provide broad figures showing that enough staff are dedicated to continuing‑airworthiness tasks.
• A table of full‑time and part‑time staff, along with total man‑hours, should be maintained.
• A training policy must be in place to keep staff qualified. | • The table can be simplified or expanded depending on the organisation’s size and complexity. | | Records & Documentation | • The CAMO must manage an aircraft continuing‑airworthiness records system and an aircraft technical log.
• Flight hours and cycles must be recorded accurately to plan maintenance.
• Records must be retained and accessible for competent‑authority inspection. | • The system should allow the CAMO to access current flight data at all times. | | Minimum Equipment List (MEL) & Maintenance Programme | • The CAMO is responsible for issuing, updating, using, and managing the MEL (if applicable).
• The maintenance programme must satisfy State‑of‑Registry requirements and be developed, updated, approved, or accepted by the CAMO.
• Sources for the programme and mandatory tasks must be clearly identified. | • The programme must be aligned with regulatory requirements. | | Subcontracting | • If any continuing‑airworthiness tasks are subcontracted, the CAMO must:
– Have procedures for controlling these arrangements.
– Include subcontractor responsibilities in the contract.
– Ensure the subcontractor informs the CAMO of any changes that affect performance.
– Maintain access to all relevant data.
– Retain the authority to override subcontractor recommendations when necessary.
– Verify that the subcontractor has qualified technical expertise and sufficient resources.
– Include competent‑authority monitoring provisions in the contract. | • The CAMO must monitor the subcontractor’s procedures and any amendments, and close any findings from competent‑authority monitoring. | Summary The CAMO’s responsibilities cover: 1. Leadership & Authority – The nominated postholder and supporting group must oversee all maintenance activities, finance them, and ensure compliance with Part‑M. 2. Human Resources – Adequate staffing and training must be documented and maintained. 3. Documentation – A robust records system, including flight hours, cycles, MEL, and maintenance programmes, must be kept and made available for inspection. 4. Subcontracting Control – When tasks are outsourced, the CAMO must contract, monitor, and retain ultimate authority over the work, ensuring that subcontractors meet all regulatory and resource requirements. These duties collectively ensure that aircraft remain airworthy, compliant, and safe throughout their operational life.
